documents.in_world_title: Helixure Biologics — Building 7 Badge Access & Escort Exception Log (2026-03-20 to 2026-03-21)
documents.owner_source: Helixure Biologics Physical Security — Lenel OnGuard Export (Subpoena Return to Cambridge PD)
documents.reveals: A raw security export shows an after-hours entry at 22:39 tied to the shared executive pool credential ‘EXEC-0441’ and a concurrent ‘ESCORT MODE ENABLED’ event tied to a QA-admin host badge. The log explicitly notes that escort mode can allow additional persons to pass controlled doors without generating their own badge events, explaining why late-night movement may not map cleanly to one person.
documents.player_inference: The 22:39 ‘EXEC-0441’ hit is not proof Evan Kline was present; it’s a shared credential used alongside escort exceptions. The killer likely exploited escort-mode/piggybacking to move through Building 7 while keeping the access trail ambiguous.
documents.document_justification: This is the case’s ‘boring spine’ document: it resolves CONTRA-01 and CONTRA-02 by showing exactly how a late-night ‘executive credential’ record can be real while still not identifying a single individual. It converts interviews (B1/B2) into a testable access-control mechanism and anchors the critical 22:39 timestamp in the investigation timeline.

production_brief_writing.summary: A tabular access-control event log exported from Helixure’s Lenel OnGuard system for Building 7 covering the evening of 2026-03-20 through the morning of 2026-03-21. It should look like a cold, timestamp-precise spreadsheet printout with a short analyst cover note that defines ‘EXEC-0441’ as a shared executive pool credential and explains ‘ESCORT MODE’ behavior in plain security-admin language.
production_brief_writing.tone: raw security system export — table-forward, minimal narrative; one short cover note written by a security systems admin
production_brief_writing.key_mandatory_line: 2026-03-20 22:39:03 — READER: L4 ELEV VESTIBULE (BLDG7) — ACCESS GRANTED — CRED: EXEC-0441 — NOTE: ESCORT MODE ENABLED (Host badge: QA-ADMIN-7719; duration: 00:15:00)
key_information_to_include: Header fields: case reference (CPD-MCU-26-0319), export request ID, date range, system name (Lenel OnGuard)
A highlighted event at 22:39:03 showing ‘EXEC-0441’ access granted at the Level 4 elevator vestibule reader
A linked system event line immediately after: ‘ESCORT MODE ENABLED’ with a host credential labeled as QA-admin (do NOT present this as a named person; present it as a badge/role label)
A short cover-note definition: EXEC-0441 is a shared executive pool credential kept at Security (not assigned to one employee)
A short cover-note definition: Escort mode allows additional entrants to pass subsequent controlled doors without their own badge events (explains why badge logs can’t always identify every body)
At least 3 additional rows within 22:39–23:36 showing controlled-door traversals (e.g., cGMP corridor reader events) that are attributable only to EXEC-0441/escort context
A mundane but telling policy footer: ‘Anti-passback disabled for EXEC pool credentials’ or similar, to underline the loophole
production_brief_writing.voice_notes: Keep the bulk of the document as machine-like rows (time, reader, event, credential, result). The only human voice is a brief cover note from a Security Systems Administrator explaining the meaning of the labels because detectives asked. Make the loophole feel real: boring, institutional, and exactly the kind of thing a planner would exploit. Mention Stairwell 4B as a nearby location in a reader/location column or in the detective highlight note, tying the log to A4’s layout without solving the whole case.
